---
title: "Reproducible Financial Time Series Analysis"
author:
  - "Sati Ter-Harutyunyan"
  - "Anna Perekhodko"
  - "Markiian Strohyi"
  - "Mykola Bolokh"
date: today
format:
  html:
    code-fold: show
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

from pathlib import Path
import sys

PROJECT_ROOT = Path.cwd()
if not (PROJECT_ROOT / "src").exists():
    PROJECT_ROOT = PROJECT_ROOT.parent

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))
```

# Introduction

This project rewrites the original R-based ATVI GARCH analysis as a
reproducible Python project. The goal is to keep the statistical workflow
transparent and runnable from a clean environment.

# Reproducibility Problems in the Original Project

The original project provides useful analysis, but several details make it hard
to reproduce directly:

- source files are tied to an R/RStudio workflow;
- paths in the R Markdown file point to the author's local machine;
- dependencies are not captured in a container or lockfile;
- data preparation, exploratory analysis, modelling, and reporting are tightly
  coupled inside the report.

This rewrite separates reusable Python code from the final report and makes the
environment reproducible with `uv`, `Makefile`, Quarto, Sphinx, and Docker.

# Data and Returns

The raw dataset is stored in `data/raw/ATVI.csv`. The processing step creates a
stable dataset in `data/processed/atvi_processed.csv` with adjusted log prices,
close-to-close log returns, percentage returns, and Garman-Klass volatility.

```{python}
#| label: data-eda-pipeline

from IPython.display import Image, display

from src.pipeline import run_data_eda

data_eda = run_data_eda()
processed = data_eda.processed_data
```

```{python}
#| label: data-summary

summary = {
    "rows": len(processed),
    "start_date": processed["Date"].min().date(),
    "end_date": processed["Date"].max().date(),
    "columns": len(processed.columns),
}
summary
```

```{python}
#| label: processed-head

processed[
    [
        "Date",
        "Open",
        "High",
        "Low",
        "Close",
        "Adjusted",
        "log_adjusted",
        "cc_ret",
        "return_pct",
        "gk_vol",
    ]
].head()
```

The processed dataset contains 1,762 trading days from 5 January 2015 to
31 December 2021. Returns are computed as close-to-close log differences of the
adjusted close price:

$$
r_t = \log(P_t^{adj}) - \log(P_{t-1}^{adj})
$$

The percentage return column is simply:

$$
100 \times r_t
$$

The Garman-Klass volatility column is included because it will later serve as an
external benchmark for evaluating fitted GARCH volatility.

# Exploratory Diagnostics

The first exploratory step is to inspect price levels and log prices. Both the
Close and Adjusted series show persistent movement rather than fluctuation
around a fixed mean. This is the standard visual pattern of a non-stationary
financial price series.

```{python}
#| label: price-panels
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(data_eda.price_plot_path)))
```

The ACF/PACF plot confirms the same point. The ACF of log adjusted prices decays
very slowly, while the PACF has a dominant first-lag spike. This is consistent
with a unit-root process.

```{python}
#| label: price-acf-pacf
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(data_eda.acf_pacf_plot_path)))
```

The Augmented Dickey-Fuller checks below follow the same two-step logic used in
the original analysis: first with a deterministic trend, then with a constant
only. In both cases we fail to reject the unit-root null at the 5% level.

```{python}
#| label: adf-summary

data_eda.adf_summary
```

This motivates the rest of the project: we do not model price levels directly.
Instead, subsequent sections focus on log returns and volatility.

# Returns Diagnostics

After confirming that price levels are non-stationary, the analysis shifts to
percentage log returns. Returns are the standard input for volatility modelling
because they fluctuate around a more stable mean than price levels.

```{python}
#| label: returns-analysis-run
#| echo: false
#| output: false

from src.returns_analysis import ReturnsAnalyzer

returns_analyzer = ReturnsAnalyzer(data=processed)

returns_analyzer.returns_plot()
returns_analyzer.acf_pacf_plot()
returns_analyzer.acf_triple_plot()
returns_analyzer.distribution_plot()

returns_ljung_box = returns_analyzer.ljung_box_summary()
returns_adf = returns_analyzer.adf_returns_summary()
returns_bds = returns_analyzer.bds_summary()
returns_arch = returns_analyzer.arch_summary()
returns_jb = returns_analyzer.jarque_bera_summary()

returns_figures_dir = returns_analyzer.figures_dir
```

```{python}
#| label: returns-plot
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(returns_figures_dir / "03_returns.png")))
```

The return series fluctuates around zero and exhibits periods of elevated
volatility followed by calmer periods. This clustering of large and small
movements is a common stylized fact of financial return series.

```{python}
#| label: returns-acf-pacf
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(returns_figures_dir / "04_returns_acf_pacf.png")))
```

The return ACF and PACF are substantially weaker than those of price levels,
although several statistically significant short-lag autocorrelations remain.
This suggests weak linear dependence in returns while still motivating a simple
conditional mean specification before volatility modelling.

```{python}
#| label: returns-ljung-box
#| echo: false

returns_ljung_box
```

The Ljung-Box tests reject the null hypothesis of no autocorrelation at the
reported lags. This suggests that the returns still contain serial dependence.

```{python}
#| label: returns-adf
#| echo: false

returns_adf
```

The ADF tests reject the unit-root null for returns under both specifications.
This supports treating the return series as stationary.

```{python}
#| label: returns-volatility-acf
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(returns_figures_dir / "05_returns_acf_triple.png")))
```

The ACFs of absolute and squared returns display persistent positive
autocorrelation with slow decay across many lags. This is strong evidence of
volatility clustering, one of the primary stylized facts of financial returns.
Large return movements tend to be followed by further large movements, while
periods of low volatility also persist over time.

```{python}
#| label: returns-arch
#| echo: false

returns_arch
```

The ARCH LM tests strongly reject the null hypothesis of no ARCH effects across
all reported lag orders. This confirms the presence of conditional
heteroskedasticity and provides direct statistical motivation for fitting
GARCH-type volatility models.

```{python}
#| label: returns-bds
#| echo: false

returns_bds
```

The BDS test rejects independence, suggesting that the return series contains
nonlinear dependence not captured by a simple independent-noise model.

```{python}
#| label: returns-distribution
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(returns_figures_dir / "06_returns_distribution.png")))
```

The histogram and Q-Q plot show substantial departures from normality,
particularly in the tails. The return distribution is more peaked around the
center and exhibits heavier tails than a Gaussian distribution, indicating
leptokurtosis and an elevated probability of extreme market movements.

```{python}
#| label: returns-jarque-bera
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd

pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "statistic": ["Jarque-Bera"],
        "value": [returns_jb[0]],
        "p_value": [returns_jb[1]],
    }
)
```

The Jarque-Bera test strongly rejects normality, supporting the conclusion that
returns are heavy-tailed and leptokurtic. This motivates the later use of
Student-t innovations rather than Gaussian errors in volatility models.

# ARMA Baseline Model

Before fitting GARCH models, we estimate a simple ARMA-style mean equation for
returns. This provides a baseline model for the conditional mean and allows the
remaining residual dependence to be inspected.

```{python}
#| label: arma-baseline-run
#| echo: false
#| output: false

from src.arma_model import ARMAModel

arma_model = ARMAModel(yret=returns_analyzer.compute_yret())

arma_result = arma_model.fit()
arma_model.residual_acf_plot(arma_result)

arma_figures_dir = arma_model.figures_dir
```

```{python}
#| label: arma-summary
#| echo: false

print(arma_result.summary())
```

The estimated ARMA mean equation captures a small but statistically significant
first-order dependence in returns. The negative AR(1) coefficient suggests mild
short-run mean reversion, although the magnitude of the effect remains limited.
The estimated Student-t degrees of freedom parameter is relatively low,
supporting the earlier evidence of heavy tails and leptokurtosis in returns.

```{python}
#| label: arma-residual-acf
#| echo: false

display(Image(filename=str(arma_figures_dir / "07_arma_residuals.png")))
```

The residual autocorrelation is substantially reduced after fitting the ARMA
mean equation. However, the absolute and squared residuals continue to display
persistent positive autocorrelation across multiple lags. This indicates that
volatility clustering and conditional heteroskedasticity remain unexplained by
the mean specification alone.

The persistence in residual volatility suggests that the dependence structure is
primarily located in the conditional variance rather than the conditional mean.
This provides direct motivation for the GARCH models introduced in the following
section.

# GARCH Models

Having established that ATVI returns exhibit strong ARCH effects and
that the ARMA model leaves variance structure unmodelled, we now fit a
sequence of GARCH-family models. All models share the same mean
specification — ARMA(1,0) with a constant and Student-t errors — and
differ only in how they model the conditional variance $\sigma^2_t$.

```{python}
#| label: garch-setup
#| output: false

from src.garch_models import GARCHModels, run_garch_models

gm = GARCHModels.from_processed_csv()
```

## sGARCH(1,1)

The standard GARCH(1,1) variance equation is:

$$
\sigma^2_t = \omega + \alpha_1 u^2_{t-1} + \beta_1 \sigma^2_{t-1}
$$

where $\omega > 0$ is the long-run variance intercept, $\alpha_1 \geq 0$
measures how strongly last period's squared shock feeds into today's
variance, and $\beta_1 \geq 0$ measures variance persistence.
Covariance stationarity requires $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$.

```{python}
#| label: sgarch-fit
#| output: true

gm.fit_sgarch()
```

The estimated $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 \approx 0.93$ confirms high
persistence — most of yesterday's conditional variance carries over to
today. The low degrees-of-freedom parameter ($\nu \approx 4.3$)
confirms the heavy tails documented in Section 3.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sgarch-acf
#| fig-cap: "sGARCH standardised residual ACF diagnostics. The ACF of z, |z|, and z² are all near white noise, confirming the variance structure is well captured."

gm.diagnostics_sgarch()
```

The sign bias test finds no significant asymmetry (all p-values > 0.52),
suggesting the symmetric sGARCH variance equation is not missing a
directional effect — though this will be revisited with the GJR model.

## GJR-GARCH(1,1)

GJR-GARCH extends sGARCH with a leverage term:

$$
\sigma^2_t = \omega + \alpha_1 u^2_{t-1} +
\gamma_1 u^2_{t-1} \cdot \mathbf{1}[u_{t-1} < 0] + \beta_1 \sigma^2_{t-1}
$$

The indicator $\mathbf{1}[u_{t-1} < 0]$ equals 1 when the previous
shock was negative (bad news). A significant $\gamma_1 > 0$ indicates
the **leverage effect**: negative shocks increase volatility more than
positive shocks of equal magnitude. This is a well-documented empirical
regularity in equity markets.

```{python}
#| label: gjrgarch-fit

gm.fit_gjrgarch()
```

The estimated $\alpha_1 \approx 0$ (not significant) and
$\gamma_1 \approx 0.135$ (highly significant) confirm asymmetry: the
entire shock reaction is concentrated in the negative side. The AIC
is lower than sGARCH, indicating a better fit despite the sign bias
test finding no asymmetry — the explicit model is more sensitive than
the residual-based test.

## Verification via fGARCH framework

```{python}
#| label: fgarch-verification
#| output: true

gm.fit_gjrgarch_fgarch()
```

Fitting GJR-GARCH via the fGARCH family framework (approximated using
EGARCH in Python, since the arch library does not provide a native
fGARCH implementation) and applying the delta-method parameter
conversion produces coefficient estimates consistent with the direct
GJR fit, confirming the two approaches are equivalent.

## T-GARCH(1,1)

T-GARCH differs from GJR by modelling the conditional **standard
deviation** $\sigma_t$ directly rather than the variance:

$$
\sigma_t = \omega + \alpha_1 |u_{t-1}| +
\gamma_1 |u_{t-1}| \cdot \mathbf{1}[u_{t-1} < 0] + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}
$$

Squaring both sides gives a nonlinear variance equation with a
different asymmetric shape to GJR-GARCH. T-GARCH is widely used in
practice and corresponds to the fGARCH TGARCH submodel in the original
R analysis.

**Implementation note:** the arch library does not provide a native
T-GARCH model. We use EGARCH(1,1,1) as an approximation that captures
the same qualitative leverage effect. The parameter values will differ
from the R output but the qualitative conclusions remain valid.

```{python}
#| label: tgarch-fit

gm.fit_tgarch()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-nic-three
#| fig-cap: "News Impact Curves for sGARCH (black), GJR-GARCH (red), and T-GARCH (blue). sGARCH is a symmetric parabola. GJR and T-GARCH are steeper for negative shocks, capturing the leverage effect."

gm.news_impact_curve_three()
```

The NIC plot makes the leverage effect visually clear. For sGARCH the
curve is symmetric — a negative and positive shock of the same size
produce identical increases in variance. For GJR and T-GARCH the left
side (negative shocks) is steeper: bad news raises volatility more
than good news of the same magnitude. This asymmetry has a practical
implication for risk management — downside moves are more destabilising
than upside moves.

```{python}
#| label: fig-tgarch-diag
#| fig-cap: "T-GARCH standardised residual diagnostics. Nyblom stability test, ACF triple, Ljung-Box, ARCH test, and BDS on log(|z|)."

gm.diagnostics_tgarch()
```

The Nyblom joint stability test passes at the 10% level, confirming
that the T-GARCH parameters are stable across the 2015–2021 sample.
The BDS test on $\log(|z|)$ finds all p-values above 0.19, confirming
that the T-GARCH standardised residuals are i.i.d. — the model has
fully absorbed the dependence structure in returns.

## NIC comparison: sGARCH vs GJR-GARCH

```{python}
#| label: fig-nic-two
#| fig-cap: "News Impact Curves for sGARCH (black) and GJR-GARCH (red). The asymmetry introduced by the gamma term is clearly visible."

gm.news_impact_curve_two()
```

## GJR-GARCH with variance targeting

Variance targeting constrains $\omega$ so that the unconditional
variance implied by the model equals the sample variance. This removes
$\omega$ from the free parameters and can improve numerical stability.

```{python}
#| label: gjrgarch-vt-fit

gm.fit_gjrgarch_vt()
```

The AIC is marginally lower than the unconstrained GJR, confirming
that variance targeting is a viable alternative with a negligible
effect on fit quality.

## IGARCH(1,1)

Integrated GARCH imposes $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 1$. This means shocks
to variance have a **permanent** effect — the conditional variance
process has a unit root and never fully reverts to a long-run level.
The mean model is reduced to ARMA(0,0) (constant only, no AR term).

```{python}
#| label: igarch-fit

gm.fit_igarch()
```

IGARCH has the highest AIC of all models fitted, indicating the
permanent-shock assumption is too strong for ATVI returns over this
sample.

## Model comparison

```{python}
#| label: tbl-model-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "AIC and BIC for all four GARCH models. T-GARCH has the lowest AIC and is selected as the preferred model for forecasting."

gm.compare_models()
```

T-GARCH achieves the lowest AIC, confirming it as the preferred model.
It captures the leverage effect, produces stable parameters across the
sample, and yields i.i.d. standardised residuals. The fitted T-GARCH
model will be used in Section 6 for volatility forecasting evaluation
and in Section 7 for the ex-ante 10-day forecast.

# Forecasting

The fitted GARCH-family models are now used to produce volatility
forecasts. Each model is passed to the `GARCHForecaster`, which generates
fixed-horizon ex-post forecasts: for a sequence of forecast origins inside
the observed sample, the one-step-ahead conditional mean and standard
deviation are recorded and paired with the return actually observed at
each target date.

```{python}
#| label: forecast-run
#| echo: false
#| output: false

from src.garch_predict import GARCHForecaster, run_forecasting

forecasts = run_forecasting(gm, horizon=1, n_expost=100, verbose=False)

forecaster = GARCHForecaster.from_garch_models(gm)
forecast_fig_path = forecaster.plot_forecast_fixed(
    forecasts["T-GARCH(1,1)"],
    title="T-GARCH ex-post forecast",
    save=True,
)
forecast_figures_dir = forecaster.figures_dir
```

The forecaster selects its method automatically from the volatility
process of each fitted model. Plain GARCH models admit analytic forecasts;
the EGARCH-based models used for GJR-GARCH and T-GARCH are forecast by
simulation under a fixed seed, keeping the results reproducible.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-forecast-tgarch
#| tbl-cap: "First ten one-step-ahead ex-post forecasts from the preferred T-GARCH model."

forecasts["T-GARCH(1,1)"].head(10)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-forecast-tgarch
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "T-GARCH one-step-ahead ex-post forecasts over the evaluation window. Observed returns (points) against the forecast mean (line) and the 95% forecast band (shaded)."

display(Image(filename=str(forecast_figures_dir / "forecast_fixed_t-garch_ex-post_forecast.png")))
```

The observed returns fall predominantly within the forecast band, and the
band contracts and expands with forecast volatility rather than staying
fixed — the behaviour expected from a working GARCH-type model.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-forecast-sigma-compare
#| tbl-cap: "Mean forecast volatility over the evaluation window for each fitted model."

pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "model": list(forecasts.keys()),
        "mean_forecast_sigma": [
            fc_table["sigma"].mean() for fc_table in forecasts.values()
        ],
    }
)
```

# Forecast Evaluation

The ex-post forecasts are now assessed quantitatively. For each model the
one-step-ahead conditional mean is compared against the observed return,
and the conditional volatility forecast is compared against a
realized-volatility proxy. Two models are then compared directly with a
test of equal predictive accuracy, and the preferred model is checked for
forecast optimality.

```{python}
#| label: evaluation-run
#| echo: false
#| output: false

import numpy as np

from src.evaluation import (
    diebold_mariano,
    evaluate_return_forecast,
    evaluate_volatility_forecast,
    mincer_zarnowitz,
    plot_mincer_zarnowitz,
)

tgarch_fc = forecasts["T-GARCH(1,1)"]
sgarch_fc = forecasts["sGARCH(1,1)"]

realized_proxy = np.abs(tgarch_fc["actual"].to_numpy())
evaluation_figures_dir = forecast_figures_dir
```

## Forecast error measures

```{python}
#| label: tbl-return-error-measures
#| tbl-cap: "Error measures for the one-step-ahead return forecast of each model. Percentage measures are undefined for a signed return series and are reported as NaN."

return_errors = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        label: evaluate_return_forecast(table["actual"], table["mean"])
        for label, table in forecasts.items()
    }
)
return_errors
```

The level error measures summarise how close each model's conditional mean
forecast lies to the observed return. The random-walk-scaled measures
(ScMAE, ScRMSE) compare each forecast against a naive previous-value
forecast: a value below one indicates the model improves on the naive
benchmark.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-volatility-error-measures
#| tbl-cap: "Error measures for the volatility forecast of each model, evaluated against the absolute return as a realized-volatility proxy."

volatility_errors = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        label: evaluate_volatility_forecast(
            np.abs(table["actual"].to_numpy()), table["sigma"]
        )
        for label, table in forecasts.items()
    }
)
volatility_errors
```

A GARCH model forecasts a conditional standard deviation that is never
directly observed, so the volatility forecast is assessed against the
absolute return as a realized-volatility proxy.

## Comparing models

```{python}
#| label: tbl-diebold-mariano
#| tbl-cap: "Diebold-Mariano test comparing the T-GARCH and sGARCH return forecasts. A negative statistic favours the first model; the Harvey-Leybourne-Newbold small-sample correction is applied."

e_tgarch = tgarch_fc["actual"].to_numpy() - tgarch_fc["mean"].to_numpy()
e_sgarch = sgarch_fc["actual"].to_numpy() - sgarch_fc["mean"].to_numpy()

dm_result = diebold_mariano(e_tgarch, e_sgarch, horizon=1, power=2)
dm_result
```

The Diebold-Mariano test assesses whether the difference in forecast
accuracy between the two models is statistically significant rather than
an artefact of the particular evaluation window.

## Forecast optimality

```{python}
#| label: tbl-mincer-zarnowitz
#| tbl-cap: "Mincer-Zarnowitz regression of the realized-volatility proxy on the T-GARCH volatility forecast. Forecast optimality corresponds to an intercept of zero and a slope of one."

mz_result = mincer_zarnowitz(realized_proxy, tgarch_fc["sigma"].to_numpy())
mz_result["coef_table"]
```

```{python}
#| label: mz-joint-test
#| echo: false

pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "statistic": ["Mincer-Zarnowitz joint F-test"],
        "f_stat": [mz_result["f_stat"]],
        "p_value": [mz_result["f_pvalue"]],
    }
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-mincer-zarnowitz
#| echo: false
#| output: false

plot_mincer_zarnowitz(
    realized_proxy,
    tgarch_fc["sigma"].to_numpy(),
    title="T-GARCH Mincer-Zarnowitz optimality",
    save=True,
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-mz-display
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Mincer-Zarnowitz scatter for the T-GARCH volatility forecast. The fitted regression line is compared against the 45-degree optimality line; a gap between the two indicates departure from forecast optimality."

display(Image(filename=str(evaluation_figures_dir / "t-garch_mincer-zarnowitz_optimality.png")))
```

The Mincer-Zarnowitz regression tests forecast optimality directly: an
optimal forecast has a zero intercept and a unit slope. The joint F-test
reports whether that hypothesis can be rejected for the T-GARCH volatility
forecast over the evaluation window.

# Reproducibility Notes

The analysis code is organized as Python modules in `src/`, while this Quarto
report imports those modules and presents the results. The full workflow can be
run from a clean environment with:

```bash
make reproduce
```

For Docker-based verification before release or presentation, the project uses:

```bash
make docker-check
```

The Docker image runs the same reproducible workflow and writes the rendered
HTML report to `outputs/report/analysis.html`. The processed dataset is
regenerated only when the source data is intentionally updated.
